attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with three months of progressive confusion and memory loss. Her family reports that she has become increasingly unsteady on her feet and developed jerking movements of her limbs. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. There is no family history of neurological disease.

On examination, she appears disorientated. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. She has myoclonic jerks in her upper limbs. Her Mini-Mental State Examination score is 12/30. Cranial nerve examination is normal. She has increased tone in all limbs with brisk reflexes.

Blood tests including full blood count, biochemistry, thyroid function, vitamin B12, and folate are normal. Magnetic resonance imaging shows cortical ribboning and bilateral basal ganglia hyperintensity on diffusion-weighted sequences.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, and characteristic MRI findings, which are highly suggestive of Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD). The rapid progression over months, rather than years, distinguishes this from other forms of dementia.

Sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is the most common human prion disease, typically affecting older adults with a median age of onset of 60-65 years. The classic triad includes rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, and characteristic EEG findings. MRI typically shows cortical ribboning and basal ganglia hyperintensity on diffusion-weighted imaging, as seen in this case.

Alzheimer disease typically has a much slower progression over years rather than months. While it can cause dementia, myoclonus is not a typical feature, and MRI findings would show hippocampal atrophy rather than cortical ribboning.

Viral encephalitis typically has a more acute onset over days to weeks, often with fever, headache, and altered consciousness. MRI would typically show temporal lobe involvement in herpes simplex encephalitis, the most common cause.

Lewy body dementia is characterised by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism. While it can present with rapid decline, the progression is typically over years rather than months, and MRI findings would be different.

Cryptococcal meningitis typically presents with headache, fever, and meningeal signs, and is most common in immunocompromised patients. MRI would show meningeal enhancement and possibly cryptococcomas, rather than the pattern seen here.

Further investigation for CJD would include EEG (which may show characteristic periodic sharp wave complexes), and CSF analysis for 14-3-3 protein, which is elevated in CJD but is not specific. The diagnosis can only be confirmed definitively by brain biopsy or post-mortem examination showing spongiform changes and abnormal prion protein deposition.
learningPoints: Sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease typically presents with rapidly progressive dementia and myoclonus in older adults.
14-3-3 protein in cerebrospinal fluid supports the diagnosis of Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease but is not specific.
references: Zerr I, Ladogana A, Mead S, Hermann P, Forloni G, Appleby BS. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease and other prion diseases. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2024;10(1):14. Published 2024 Feb 29. doi:10.1038/s41572-024-00497-y
